summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Left knee pain and swelling.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports left knee pain and swelling, primarily in both knees, which has worsened with stair workouts. He attributes this to increased activity. He has a prior right shoulder rotator cuff repair two years ago with no subsequent issues. He reports no other joint pain and denies fever, chills, or nausea. He takes norvasc 5 mg daily for hypertension and monitors his blood sugars, which have been well-controlled. He has been taking medication for hypertension and the joint pain has not elevated.

Findings
Physical examination reveals mild erythema and edema in the left knee with a small effusion. Range of motion is decreased in the left knee. The patient's blood pressure is elevated today. The neck is supple, thyroid is not enlarged, lungs are clear, and the abdomen is soft. White blood cell count is normal. The a1c is slightly elevated.

Assessment
Left knee pain and swelling, likely secondary to a strain.

Plan
Order autoimmune labs to evaluate for underlying inflammatory conditions. Order a physical therapy referral. Prescribe meloxicam 15 mg daily for pain relief. Order a lipid profile. Continue metformin 500 mg twice daily. Instruct the patient to monitor blood pressure at home and report any changes. Schedule a follow-up appointment in four weeks to assess response to treatment.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Joint pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Janet Sullivan is a 33-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for evaluation of joint pain.

She localizes the joint pain to her bilateral knees. The patient reports she has been doing stair workouts, which she thought was building muscle on her knees. She denies any pins and needles in her feet or swelling in her legs. The patient notes that she would look out for those symptoms because she has diabetes. She denies any fever or chills. She denies any nausea or vomiting. The patient denies any chest pain or shortness of breath.

She notes she had a right shoulder rotator cuff repair about 2 years ago, but she has not had any problems since that point.

She reports that her diabetes are well-controlled.

The patient does have high blood pressure but she monitors it and takes Norvasc 5 mg a day. She notes her blood pressure has not been elevated since her joint pain started.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Constitutional: Negative for fever, chills
• Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain or lower leg swelling.
• Respiratory: Negative for shortness of breath.
• Gastrointestinal: Negative for nausea, vomiting.
• Musculoskeletal: Positive for Arthralgias

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Neck
• General Examination: Neck is supple without thyromegaly.

Respiratory
• Auscultation of Lungs: Clear bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular
• Auscultation of Heart: Regular rate and rhythm.

Musculoskeletal
• Left knee: Slight erythema and edema. Small effusion present. Decreased flexion and extension. Strength in lower extremities is good.

RESULTS

Views: Four views left knee.
Indication: Knee pain.
Interpretation: There are no fractures, dislocations, or other abnormalities.

PLAN

Ms. Janet Sullivan is a 33-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for evaluation of joint pain.

Left knee strain.
• Medical Treatment: We will order an autoimmune panel for further workup. I am going to refer her to physical therapy and prescribe meloxicam 15 mg once daily to help with pain.
• Specialist Referrals: Physical therapy.

Hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: Her blood pressure is elevated in office today, but has been well-controlled on Norvasc 5 mg daily based on home monitoring.
• Medical Treatment: Continue current regimen and home monitoring. She will send me a report in the next couple of weeks. We will also order a lipid profile.

Diabetes type II.
• Medical Reasoning: Her hemoglobin a1C is slightly elevated, but her blood glucose levels have been well-controlled.
• Medical Treatment: Continue metformin 500 mg twice a day.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Left knee pain and swelling.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports left knee pain and swelling, primarily in both knees, which has worsened with stair workouts. He attributes this to increased activity. He has a prior right shoulder rotator cuff repair two years ago with no subsequent issues. He reports no other joint pain and denies fever, chills, or nausea. He takes norvasc 5 mg daily for hypertension and monitors his blood sugars, which have been well-controlled. He has been taking medication for hypertension and the joint pain has not elevated.
Findings
Physical examination reveals mild erythema and edema in the left knee with a small effusion. Range of motion is decreased in the left knee. The patient's blood pressure is elevated today. The neck is supple, thyroid is not enlarged, lungs are clear, and the abdomen is soft. White blood cell count is normal. The a1c is slightly elevated.
Assessment
Left knee pain and swelling, likely secondary to a strain.
Plan
Order autoimmune labs to evaluate for underlying inflammatory conditions. Order a physical therapy referral. Prescribe meloxicam 15 mg daily for pain relief. Order a lipid profile. section_miscategorizations.text: He takes norvasc 5 mg daily for hypertension and monitors his blood sugars, which have been well-controlled.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Blood pressure and blood sugar control status are more in the realm of overall medical history/assessment than the focused knee HPI, but including them in HPI as relevant background is acceptable and not misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: He has been taking medication for hypertension and the joint pain has not elevated.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is somewhat awkwardly phrased and mixes chronic disease management with the acute complaint, but it still reflects patient-reported history and is acceptable in HPI.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's blood pressure is elevated today.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is an objective vital sign and correctly placed in Findings; no recategorization needed, but it might also be summarized in Assessment. Placement here is not misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: The neck is supple, thyroid is not enlarged, lungs are clear, and the abdomen is soft.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are general exam findings and appropriately belong in Findings; they are somewhat extraneous to the knee complaint but not miscategorized.

section_miscategorizations.text: White blood cell count is normal. The a1c is slightly elevated.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are lab results and correctly placed in Findings; A1c could also inform Assessment, but its current placement is acceptable.
This is a treatment decision (to continue an existing medication) and belongs in the Plan; although metformin was not mentioned earlier, its placement here is still correct.
